author: @grandmasterflash213
text: Hi Robert, I’m a mechanical engineer in the UK for a major manufacturing company. I implemented these split anti-backlash gears as a direct replacement, they improved the process immediately! I love your channel by the way!!

author: @jeroenkoehorst4056
text: Thanks! Is efficiency a downside of this system when used in an actual setup out of steel?

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
Efficiency is just about never a downside, its a little harder to acheive though sometimes.
Here, usually utilisation of a worm drive usually has the effect of a loss in speed, you can't have speed, good torque and precision all at once easily.

author: @marcoviniciomorafallas1719
text:
Hi, thanks for your video!
You mentioned that gears can jam without backlash. Has anyone ever designed a gear-based brake that works by using backlash—or other meshing properties—to stop motion?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Not that I know of

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @GabrielPettier
text: Aaed Musa did a couple interesting videos on capstan drives and building a robot with them, it's an ingenious system that uses ropes to avoid the backlash problem, though you need a rope that doesn't stretch over time if you want that property to last, but he found one! His work is really high quality, definitely recommend watching it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: seen it mate cheers - of course the problem with that kind of drive is it doesn't actually rotate all the way round - it's more of an actuator than a drive

author: @perkmeister1
text: Just as an aside, the Oldham coupler is also sometimes used on stepper motors that drive the lead screw of a 3D printer. The idea is to try to reduce or eliminate the lateral forces on a gantry due to slightly bent lead screws (which are all 'slightly' bent to some degree). Another solution is to use a universal joint instead, and a gimble on the gantry nut. This actualy works very well, it was one of the first mods I did to my printer (actually there's enough give in the gantry nut to allow for a gimbling action, I didn't bother to use one).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice aside mate - thanks for taking the time to post it

(micro and nano motion for a 3D printer with replicator level upgrades for making ICs and such will absolutely require something like this XD)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I did see it mate and thought it was a really valid point - truth is - I am unlikely to use gears at all in the final version - but it was still a good idea to talk about it

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering ah cool, yeah I had heard of a number of ideas for anti-backlash worm drives or gears, one used a set of ball bearings like the ball nut and ballscrew that use ballbearings to create the contact between the nut and a threaded rod with round threads for half the balls to fit between them, Personally I figured the worm gear could be split in half and have the springloaded pressure causing constant contact with the worm regardless of direction by one half of the gear or another :)  

**to anyone wondering how that can be antibbacklash, the balls are jammed in so any side to side slack is causing the balls to move to the side a little filling it completely with each ball partially to one side making contact or  the other but the ball bearings are all always touching each other to keep this widening of their path they travel

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @Moist_yet_Crispy
text:
Loved the video! Hey Rob, There a project that seems promising. What's your opinion of this project. 
Video: Unlimited Energy in My Garage (No Patents) 
Channel: Limitless Potential Technologies
I usually don't pay attention to these projects but this one looks promising and they're covering legit concepts. Sending good vibes mate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I hate to comment on this sort of stuff - the guy seems genuine - why should I try and pull him to bits in something he honestly believes in. Having said that I wouldn't invest in it! And by that I mean time or money - but we all believe in what we believe in and he seems to believe it.

replies.author: @Moist_yet_Crispy
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering He does and he seems to be honest about it which I find rare enough. I always appreciate your honesty and experience. I know you're a prominent Wizard in the field. I was just curious if there was anything new about this one I guess.

replies.author: @Ferd414
replies.text: @Moist_yet_Crispy Let's put it this way: *ANYTHING* - I don't care what it is - gear systems, weights, pulleys motors, generators, *ANYTHING* - that professes to offer a greater amount of energy than what's put into it is a lie. Period. No exceptions. Ever. It doesn't matter how it's explained, (or more likely, "handwaviumed" and/or baffle-gabbed), it doesn't matter how smart/educated/genius the person making the claim, it makes no difference how convincing the explanation is, or how cool the machinery looks, there's one inviolable rule: The energy you will extract from a system will ALWAYS be less than the energy you put into it. How much less will be a function of bearings and tolerances, and various other factors, and might be anything from tiny to gigantic, but the rule holds for EVERY mechanism or process - You get out the energy you put in, or less. NEVER more. Claims to the contrary are wishful thinking, bad measurements, just plain delusions, poor procedures, recording errors, and/or outright lies designed to separate you from your money. "There ain't no such thing as a free lunch", may seem a cutesy phrase, but it's true - To produce energy (or more accurately, convert it from one form to another)requires energy, and the energy produced will always be equal to or less than the energy consumed to make it due to losses (noticed or not) in the system, however carefully designed and constructed that system may be.

author: @troncooo409
text: Sorry i watched the videos, but I don't understand how you removed the backlash of the worm driver. You've put the spring somewhere, but packing a piece here and there doesnt help me. Thanks for trying.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it forces the gear to mesh on the left or the right depending on the way it turns -  backlash is really just the taking up of this gap - the spring makes the gear tooth do that and the backlash is removed
